1.synthesis: Hearing that Jacob is ill, Joseph brings his two sons — Manasseh and Ephraim — to receive their grandfather's blessing (vv.1–2). Jacob rallies his strength and sits up on the bed. He begins with covenant recitation: God Almighty (El Shaddai) appeared to him at Luz/Bethel and promised to make him fruitful and to give Canaan to his descendants as an everlasting possession (vv.3–4). Then the legal act: 'And now your two sons, who were born to you in the land of Egypt before I came to you in Egypt, are mine; Ephraim and Manasseh shall be mine, as Reuben and Simeon are mine' (v.5). Joseph's Egyptian-born sons are adopted into the tribal structure as full sons of Jacob, thereby receiving full tribal inheritance portions rather than the secondary share they would have as a single tribe named 'Joseph.' Jacob closes with a poignant memory: Rachel's death near Bethlehem, which robbed her of seeing this moment (v.7).
1.historical_context: The adoption of Ephraim and Manasseh as Jacob's own sons, rather than as Joseph's sons who would inherit through Joseph, has the effect of doubling Joseph's tribal inheritance: instead of one tribe-share (Joseph), two tribe-shares (Ephraim and Manasseh) represent the house of Joseph. This is the tribal-territorial reality that appears throughout the conquest and settlement: Ephraim and Manasseh have separate allotments while Levi (the priestly tribe, without territorial inheritance) is supported by the others. The Bethel-theophany recitation in vv.3–4 grounds the adoption in covenant: Jacob is not making a legal decision based on sentiment alone but invoking the Abrahamic promise that frames the entire family structure. The invocation of El Shaddai — the name used at the covenant-expansion moments in Genesis (17:1; 35:11) — signals the adoption as covenant action.
1.literary_structure: The adoption scene (vv.1–7) and the blessing scene (vv.8–22) are the two acts of Genesis 48. The adoption legally constitutes Ephraim and Manasseh as Jacob's sons; the blessing distributes the inheritance between them. Jacob's invocation of Rachel's memory (v.7) at the moment of adopting Joseph's sons is not accidental: Rachel died so that Benjamin could be born; now Jacob ensures that Joseph's sons receive a full patriarchal inheritance, compensating in some measure for what Joseph lost during the Egyptian years. The adoption is Jacob's gift to Joseph — not the garment or the double portion of ordinary firstborn-blessing but the extraordinary act of making his sons his brothers.
1.theological_application: Gill on the adoption: 'And now thy two sons, Ephraim and Manasseh — I now adopt them into my own family, and they shall have their place among my children, as if they had been born of me … this Jacob did partly out of affection to Joseph, to give him a double portion, as was due to the firstborn, and as was afterwards given him.' Matthew Henry: 'Jacob here doth that which was not common, he adopted Joseph's sons, and took them to be as his own; hereby he gave Joseph a double portion in Canaan.' The adoption of Ephraim and Manasseh is a grace act: Jacob gives Joseph what he cannot give himself — a doubled share in the promised land. The pattern is the divine adoption described in Romans 8:15: God gives his people the 'spirit of adoption as sons' through Christ, the firstborn among many brothers.
1.christology: Jacob's adoption of Ephraim and Manasseh 'as Reuben and Simeon are mine' (v.5) performs in miniature what God performs through Christ on a cosmic scale: those who are not by nature sons of Abraham are made so by the word of the Father. The Ephraim and Manasseh adoption is the patriarchal foundation for Paul's argument in Galatians 3:29: 'if you are Christ's, then you are Abraham's offspring, heirs according to promise.' The inheritance of the promised land — secured for Ephraim and Manasseh by adoption — is fulfilled for all believers through adoption into the covenant family. Jacob's recitation of the Bethel covenant (El Shaddai, vv.3–4) grounds the adoption in promise rather than sentiment: the adopted sons receive the promise along with the name.

8.synthesis: Jacob's eyes are dim with age, so Joseph presents his sons close up. Jacob kisses and embraces them, then says: 'I never expected to see your face; and behold, God has let me see your offspring also' (v.11). Joseph positions Manasseh (firstborn) at Jacob's right hand and Ephraim (younger) at his left. But Jacob crosses his hands — right on Ephraim, left on Manasseh — and blesses them with the Abrahamic formula (vv.15–16). Joseph tries to correct him, assuming it is an error of dim sight. Jacob refuses: 'I know, my son, I know. He also shall become a people, and he also shall be great. Nevertheless, his younger brother shall be greater than he, and his offspring shall become a multitude of nations' (v.19). The deliberate reversal of the firstborn's priority — now the third such reversal in Genesis (Isaac over Ishmael, Jacob over Esau, Ephraim over Manasseh) — is the pattern of sovereign grace.
8.historical_context: The blessing formula of vv.15–16 is one of the most theologically dense in Genesis: Jacob invokes 'the God before whom my fathers Abraham and Isaac walked, the God who has been my shepherd all my life long to this day, the angel who has redeemed me from all evil.' The 'angel who has redeemed' is the same divine presence who wrestled with Jacob at the Jabbok, who appeared at Bethel — the pre-incarnate Son. The verb 'redeemed' (gāʾal) is the technical term for the kinsman-redeemer, the one who rescues a relative from bondage or danger. Jacob on his deathbed confesses that his entire life has been overseen by a divine Kinsman who has bought him back from every danger. The Ephraim-over-Manasseh precedence is confirmed throughout Israelite history: Ephraim becomes the dominant northern tribe, giving its name to the entire northern kingdom.
8.literary_structure: The crossed-hands blessing (vv.13–20) is the narrative climax of ch.48, recapitulating and resolving the younger-over-elder pattern that runs through Genesis. Jacob's deliberate crossing of hands — against Joseph's expectation — makes explicit what the earlier reversals (Isaac, Jacob, Joseph himself as the eleventh son exalted above his brothers) have demonstrated: God's election does not follow the natural order of primogeniture. The blessing's three-part invocation (the God of Abraham and Isaac; my shepherd all my life; the angel who redeemed me) is a compressed autobiography of faith: the inherited covenant, the personal experience, and the divine rescue. Jacob's 'I know, my son, I know' (v.19) asserts prophetic intention, not senile confusion.
8.theological_application: Gill on the three-part blessing: 'the Angel which redeemed me from all evil — which is to be understood not of a created angel, but of the Angel of the covenant, the Son of God, who appeared in human form to the patriarchs, and who became the Redeemer of his people from sin, Satan, and death.' Matthew Henry: 'Jacob blessed them with a prayer for them: God make you as Ephraim and Manasseh! And so it was used among the Jews in blessing their children.' The blessing formula of v.20 — 'by you Israel will pronounce blessings, saying, God make you like Ephraim and Manasseh' — entered the Jewish liturgy for blessing sons on Shabbat. The crossed-hands insistence ('I know, my son, I know') is Jacob's final prophetic act: divine sovereignty over human expectation, grace over primogeniture.
8.christology: 'The angel who has redeemed me from all evil' (v.16) — Jacob's deathbed identification of his deliverer as the 'angel who redeemed' is the OT's sharpest pre-incarnate Christological statement in the patriarchal narratives. The verb gāʾal (to act as kinsman-redeemer) is the technical term for the Boaz-Ruth pattern of redemption: a close relative who pays the price to buy another out of bondage. Jacob confesses that his entire life has been redeemed by a divine Kinsman. This is the theological logic Peter applies to Christ: 'you were ransomed … not with perishable things such as silver or gold, but with the precious blood of Christ' (1 Peter 1:18–19). The younger-over-elder pattern (Ephraim over Manasseh) is Paul's election theology: God 'chooses what is foolish in the world to shame the wise' (1 Corinthians 1:27).
